segments.s300-g233: This is a noun phrase + simile followed by the verb كنيم (1st person plural). 'همه' modifies 'روی ایران' to mean 'the whole/entire surface of Iran.' Word order is topical: 'همه روی ایران' (the whole face of Iran) comes before the comparative marker 'چو' + 'دریا' ('like a sea'), and the verb 'کنیم' is the 1st-person-plural optative/imperative-like form ('let us make' / 'we will make') that applies to the whole preceding phrase.
segments.s300-g234: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by means of.' 'خورشید تابان' is a noun + adjective ('the shining sun'), and 'ثریا کنیم' repeats the same verb 'کنیم' (1st-person-plural) so the whole clause reads as an instrumental simile: 'by/from the shining sun let us make (it) the Pleiades.' The verb remains optative/ hortative ('let us make').
segments.s300-g235: 'دو شاه و دو کشور' is a coordinated noun phrase ('two kings and two countries/realms'). 'چنان' ('so/thus') modifies the following predicate-like adjective/noun 'رزمساز' (literally 'war‑maker' / 'battle‑ready' / 'warrior'), giving the sense 'so prepared for war' or 'such war‑minders.' The phrase functions as the subject description before the verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s300-g236: 'به' is the preposition 'to/towards,' here introducing the destination 'لشکرگه' ('army camp'). 'خوی' + the clitic 'ش' together form 'خویش' ('their own'); the clitic 'ش' is the 3rd-person possessive. 'رفتند' is the simple past 3rd-person-plural ('they went' / 'they returned'), and 'باز' is an adverb placed after the verb meaning 'back/again' (so 'رفتند باز' = 'they went back/they returned').
segments.s300-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic/special past/preterite-like verb meaning here 'raised/struck out/appeared' (poetic usage). سر 'head, summit' follows the verb as the direct object/phrase 'raised its head'. از introduces the source 'from', and برج گاو is a genitive phrase 'the tower/sign of the Bull' (i.e. the Taurus constellation); word order is Verb + Object + از + location phrase, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s300-g2: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from the plain'. هامون is a poetic noun 'plain/field'. برآمد is the intransitive past 'arose/issued forth'. خروش is the event noun 'a cry/roar' and چکاو names the singer/bird (the lark); the noun phrase is therefore 'from the plain arose the lark's cry.'
segments.s300-g3: تبیره is the nominal subject here (a kind of cry/announcement in context; glossed here as 'outcry/notice'), followed by برآمد 'arose'. ز again marks origin 'from'. هر دو سرای literally 'each/both sarāy(ā)' — here 'both choirs/places of singing' — so the clause reads 'a tabireh/cry rose from both choirs.' Note the poetic noun order: Subject + verb + ز + source.
segments.s300-g4: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that very'. ناله is 'a wail/lamenation'. بوق is 'horn/trumpet' and با is the preposition 'with'. کرنای here is the instrument/sonic noun 'trumpet-blast' or 'trumpet' in the instrumental phrase. The whole phrase strings together: 'the same lamentation — horn with trumpet', i.e. the identical wailing from horns and trumpets.
segments.s300-g5: ز 'from'. گردان is a military unit 'division/troop'. شمشیرزن is a compound noun 'swordsman' (here functioning as a postpositive modifier with the numeral following: 'a troop of thirty thousand swordsmen'—Persian allows numeral sequences after the counted noun in poetic diction).
segments.s300-g6: بیاورد is a narrative verb in past tense 'he brought' (classical/poetic presentational past). جهن is a proper name (person brought). از marks motion 'out of/from', در here means 'gate/entrance' (not the prepositional 'in'), and کارزار is the battlefield; together: 'brought J(h)an from the gate of the battlefield.'
segments.s300-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the subject name 'Khosrow'. بر آن گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner/so', where بر functions as a prepositional particle 'on/in' + آن 'that' + گونه 'manner'. بر before the verb (بر دیدشان) strengthens the verb as 'looked upon/seen' and دیدشان is the past verb 'he saw them' (ـشان = 'them'). Overall: 'When Khosrow, seeing them in that way...'
segments.s300-g8: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said (formally)'. تا here introduces the subordinate infinitival/purpose clause 'so that / (he ordered) to ...' and is followed by قارن کاویان: a personal name/title phrase 'Qaren (of) the Kayanian' (object of the ordering). The verb completing the purpose is implied; the line means 'he gave the order to Qaren, the Kayanian.'
segments.s300-g97: بر is the preposition 'on/upon' placed before its noun. میسره here is a proper name/place (not covered by the provided senses), so no listed sense fits. شیر جنگی is a compound: شیر (lion) + جنگی (warlike/fighter) => a poetic epithet 'war‑lion' or 'warlike lion' modifying the following name. طبرد appears as a proper name (Tabard/Tabrd) or an archaic term; grammatically it functions as the name being described by the preceding epithets.
segments.s300-g98: بشد is the past/become sense 'it became / he became' (classical past). تیز is an adjective here meaning 'swift'. با is the preposition 'with', introducing companhia. نامداران is a plural noun 'the famed/renowned ones'. گرد in this context marks association — 'together/with' or 'around' — so the sequence says that he 'became swift with / joined the renowned'.
segments.s300-g99: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the personal name 'Kai Khosrow'. آن is the demonstrative 'that', pointing to the following noun. رزم is 'battle' and ترکان is the genitive/connected noun 'the Turks' (i.e. 'the battle of the Turks'). بدید is the past of دیدن 'he saw' (classical past form).
segments.s300-g100: که here introduces a result/comment clause 'so that/that' or simply links the remark; خورشید is 'the sun'. گشت is the past/become verb 'became' or 'turned (into)' in classical usage. از is the preposition 'from/out of'. جهان is 'the world'. ناپدید is the adjective 'vanished/gone' describing the sun's state — together: 'the sun became vanished from the world'.
segments.s300-g101: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. آوه is here a place name (Aveh) and does not match the provided lexical senses, so marked -1. و is the coordinating 'and'. سمکنان is likewise a place name (Samangan) and is not represented by the boxed senses, so -1. کرد is the past of کردن 'made/did' and combines with روی to mean 'turned (his) face' — کرد + روی = 'he turned his face'. روی is the noun 'face' (here 'turned his face toward ...').
segments.s300-g102: که introduces a subordinate clause 'where/for/that'. بودند is the past plural of بودن 'they were'. شیران is used poetically for 'lions' or 'valiant men'; here it denotes fierce fighters (sense chosen as 'brave men'). پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/hostile', describing those lions/warriors.
segments.s300-g103: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'so that / in order that' (linking the command to its intended result). بر here has the directional sense 'toward/to' when combined with سوی. سوی is 'toward'. میسره again is a place/name (Mi‑sereh) and does not match the provided lexical senses, so -1.
segments.s300-g104: بتابند is a present/jussive plural form from تافتن/تابیدن here meaning 'they should shine/turn/bend' — in context a jussive sense 'let them turn/shine'. چون is the simile 'like/as'. آفتاب is 'the sun' used in a simile. از is the preposition 'from'. بره in this line is most plausibly a place/name (Bareh/Barah) rather than the literal 'lamb' given the context; the listed senses do not include the place sense, but grammatically it is the object of از.
segments.s300-g161: بدآنگه (a compound adverb) = “at that ill-omened / ill-timed moment”; که شد introduces a subordinate temporal clause meaning “when / at which it became.” چشمه سوی نشیب is a noun phrase with چشمه “spring” followed by سوی + نشیب “toward the descent,” so the clause literally says ‘when the spring turned toward the slope.’ Note the poetic order: the temporal clause marker که شد precedes the descriptive phrase.
segments.s300-g162: دل شاه ترکان is a genitive chain meaning “the heart of the king of the Turks.” بجست is a past, intransitive verb here meaning “leapt / sprang” (not ‘sought’). از نهیب is an ablative/causal phrase “from / at the (thunderous) cry,” giving the cause of the heart's leap.
segments.s300-g163: ز introduces an ablative/partitive meaning “from / out of.” جوش here means a surge or ardor (metaphorical ‘surge’ of riders). سواران is the head noun “horsemen,” and هر کشوری is a postposed modifier meaning “of each country / every realm.” The whole phrase = “from the surge of horsemen of every country.”
segments.s300-g164: A coordinated series introduced again by ز “from”: ز هر مرز و هر بوم و هر مهتری lists sources/origins in parallel (ablative): “from every border, every homeland, and every chieftain.” Each هر repeats for emphasis; the nouns are simple nominatives used in an ablative sense with ز.
segments.s300-g165: سواران شمشیر زن is a noun + attributive compound: شمشیر (noun “sword”) + زن (verbal element “strike/wield”) together function as an adjective meaning “sword‑wielding.” سی هزار (the number) follows poetically after the noun phrase to give the quantity: “thirty thousand (horsemen).”
segments.s300-g166: گزیده سوارن is an adjectival phrase “chosen / select riders” (گزیده modifying سوارن). خنجر گزار is another compound where خنجر “dagger” combines with گزار to form an agentive descriptor (“dagger‑bearer / dagger‑wielder”); گزار here is an archaic/poetic agentive element. The segment names a picked, elite body of riders described as carrying daggers.
segments.s300-g167: دگرگونه modifies جوشن: “armor of a different kind.” دگرگون modifies درفش: “the banner changed / transformed.” The pair of adjectives (دگرگونه / دگرگون) is a poetic parallel: one describes the armor, the other the banner.
segments.s300-g168: جهانی شده is a participial construction: جهانی “universal / whole” + شده “become,” meaning “became universal / widespread.” It is followed by a coordinate list of colors سرخ و زرد و بنفش, describing how the scene/banners/armors appeared — “became red, yellow, and purple.”
segments.s300-g193: دل شاه ترکان = literally “the heart of the king of the Turks.” Persian uses nouns in this genitive sequence (possessor after possessed) so دل (heart) is followed by شاه ترکان (the king of the Turks). پر از + [noun] is an adjectival phrase meaning “full of …,” so پر از خشم و جوش describes the inner state (anger and surge/turmoil).
segments.s300-g194: ز تندی نبودش به گفتار گوش = a short, idiomatic clause: ز تندی “because of/through his fierceness/haste,” نبودش is the old-style predicate literally “there was not for him” (نبود + enclitic -ش = “he lacked”), and به گفتار گوش = “an ear for speech” (به + گفتار marks target/benefit; گوش = ear/attention). Together: “because of his fierceness he had no ear for speech.”
segments.s300-g195: برانگیخت اسب از میان سپاه places the verb before its object (common in epic Persian): برانگیخت (roused/spurred) governs اسب (horse), and از میان سپاه = “out of/from among the army,” a prepositional phrase indicating origin.
segments.s300-g196: بیامد دمان با درفش سیاه is a compact descriptive line: بیامد (came) is the past tense verb; دمان (دامان) here is used poetically for the fore-edge/forefront or approach (literally “hem/skirt” but used spatially), and با درفش سیاه = “with a black standard.” Word order places the verb before the noun-phrase resultantly making the arrival vivid.
segments.s300-g197: از ایرانیان چند نامی بکشت: از ایرانیان marks the victims’ group (“of the Iranians”); چند نامی = “several/notable ones” (چند = a few/several, نامی = famous/notable). بکشت is an archaic past form meaning “he killed” (here placed after the object phrase, typical of Persian epic word order).
segments.s300-g198: چو خسرو بدید اندر آمد به پشت: چو = “when”; خسرو is the subject; بدید is the past tense “saw”; اندر آمد = “came in/forward” (اندر + آمد is a common compound meaning ‘came into the scene’); به پشت = “to the rear/behind” (indicating direction or position). The clause means that upon seeing this, Khosrow came to the rear (i.e., moved up behind).
segments.s300-g199: دو شاه دو کشور چنین کینه دار uses repetition (دو شاه دو کشور) for emphasis: “two kings of two countries/realms.” چنین = “such/so” and کینه‌دار is a compound (کینه + دار) meaning “possessing a grudge” or “full of hatred” (دار here functions as the possessive-forming element ‘-holding’ rather than a literal noun). Word order is noun phrases followed by the descriptive adjective.
segments.s300-g200: برفتند با خوار مایه سوار: برفتند is the past plural ‘they departed/rode off.’ با introduces their accompaniment (“with”); خوار مایه سوار is a compact noun group describing the force — خوار = “despised/lowly,” مایه here = “a contingent/amount” (resource/force), and سوار = “horsemen.” The phrase means they departed with a small/mean band of cavalry.
segments.s300-g105: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past tense (they went/they departed). The prepositional phrase با نامور ده هزار follows the verb by poetic inversion: با + NP ('with the famed ten-thousand') where نامور (renowned) qualifies the numeral phrase; the adjective/order is inverted for meter so the sense is 'the famed ten thousand.'
segments.s300-g106: زره‌دار here refers to armoured warriors (a noun) and is understood collectively (plural) even though formally singular; با introduces what they carry or have ('with'), and گرزه گاوسار is a noun phrase 'the mace Gausar' where گاوسار is a proper name modifying گرزه. The construction therefore means 'armored men with the mace called Gausar.'
segments.s300-g107: بفرمود is an honorific-past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; شاه (the king) is the subject, placed after the verb by poetic inversion. به + شماخ سوری is the indirect object/recipient phrase 'to Shamak Suri' (به = 'to/for'), placed before the verb for emphasis or meter.
segments.s300-g108: که introduces a content clause (that/so that) explaining the king's command. از is the partitive 'from/among,' so از نامداران ایران سپاه literally means 'an army from the renowned (men) of Iran'—سپاه is the head noun 'army' and the phrase is left as the object/complement of the command.
segments.s300-g109: گزین کن is an imperative formed from گزین (choose) plus the emphatic/imperative کن; together they mean 'choose!' or 'select!'. ز is the poetic short form of از meaning 'from,' so ز جنگ آوران marks the source 'from the warriors/fighters'; ده‌هزار is the numeral 'ten thousand' and is the quantity being commanded.
segments.s300-g110: سواران ('horsemen/riders') names the unit type; گرد here functions as a collective/assembling marker ('a body/group of'), so سواران گرد = 'a body of horsemen.' از در کارزار uses از (from/ drawn out of) + در (in/at) + کارزار (battlefield) to indicate these horsemen are drawn from the field of battle.
segments.s300-g111: میان دو صف تیغها is a prepositional phrase 'between the two ranks the swords' (تیغها = 'the swords') placed before the verb for emphasis. برکشید (here split as بر + کشید) is the past tense 'they drew/raised'—بر as a verbal prefix plus کشید (past of کشیدن) yields 'drew (swords up/on).' The verb is plural subject-agreeing (they).
segments.s300-g112: مبینید is an authorial present 'you see/behold' (narrative aside). کس را is the indefinite object 'someone' plus the definite/object marker را, سر is 'head', اندر is the classical/prepositional 'in/into', and کشید (same root as above) is a past verb here meaning 'drew/struck off'—together 'you see that someone's head was struck off' (passive/result implied by context).
segments.s300-g25: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بر دامن کوه is a prepositional phrase 'on the skirt/foothills of the mountain' (بر + noun + noun as modifier), and بنشست is a simple past verb 'sat' (3rd person singular); ماه, the logical subject 'the moon', follows the verb — a common poetic word order (V then subject).
segments.s300-g26: ایلان is the plural subject 'the heroes'. بازگشتند is the past plural verb 'returned'. ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from', and آوردگاه is the place noun 'battlefield'; the prepositional phrase 'ز آوردگاه' gives the origin of their return.
segments.s300-g27: از ایرانیان here expresses the cause or source of the emotion ('because of the Iranians'). شاد شد is a two-word past predicate 'became glad' (شاد = adjective predicative, شد = past auxiliary), and شهریار ('the king') appears after the predicate for poetic emphasis.
segments.s300-g28: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/for' explaining the reason. چیره شد(ند) is a causative/resultive idea 'they became dominant/overcame' with شدند as the past plural auxiliary, and اندر آن کارزار is an archaic locative phrase 'in that battle' (اندر = in/within).
segments.s300-g29: همه شب is an adverbial phrase 'all night'. همی is an archaic/imperfective marker indicating continued action ('kept on'), جنگ را is the definite object marked by را, and ساختند (literally 'made') here means 'kept up' or 'kept in order' — i.e. they maintained the fight through the night.
segments.s300-g30: به خواب و به خوردن are dative/target phrases 'to sleep and to eating' (i.e. toward sleeping or eating). نپرداختند is the negated past verb (ن‑ prefix + پرداختند) meaning 'they did not attend to/turn to' those activities; negative prefix plus past tense marks that they refrained from sleeping or eating.
segments.s300-g31: چو is again 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning roughly 'fell/was struck' (here taken as a past action). The phrase برزد سر places the verb before سر ('the head'), and از چنگ خرچنگ هور is a possessive/agent phrase 'from the claw of the crab Hoor' (چنگ = claw, خرچنگ = crab, هور treated as a proper name attached).
segments.s300-g32: جهان شد is 'the world became'; پر از is the idiom 'full of' followed by a coordinated list جنگ و آهنگ و شور ('war and melody/music and tumult/passion'). شد is past tense 'became', so the whole phrase means 'the world became full of ...'.
segments.s300-g121: بجنبید is a 2nd-person plural imperative ('move!' / 'stir!'). It is followed by the prepositional phrase با رستم ('with Rostam'), so the command scopes to a group moving together with Rostam. از قلبگاه ('from the heart/center') is another prepositional phrase that gives the origin/place of movement; قلبگاه is a compound noun meaning 'heart' or 'core/center.'
segments.s300-g122: This short noun cluster names the place/party left: منوشان is a proper name (Manūshān) and خوزان qualifies it as belonging to or associated with the Khuz (Khuzan). لشکر پناه is a noun phrase literally 'army + refuge' — here best read as an appositive identifying that locality as the Khuzan army's refuge/protection. The verb is omitted (a typical narrative ellipsis), so the whole segment supplies the origin/place phrase started by از قلبگاه.
segments.s300-g123: برآمد is a perfective/preterite verb meaning 'arose/issued forth.' It takes the verbal noun خروشیدن ('roaring') as a complement, so the construction means 'a roaring arose' or 'they began to roar.' بوق و کوس are the instruments that produce the sound (horn/trumpet and war-drum). Word order (verb + verbal noun + objects) is common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s300-g124: به یک دست is a locative phrase meaning 'to/at one side' (literally 'to one hand') and introduces who is positioned there. خسرو and سپهدار طوس are listed after it as the people on that side; سپهدار is a title ('commander') modifying طوس (Tūs). There is no additional linking verb — the line implies 'stood' or 'were placed' on that side.
segments.s300-g125: بیاراسته here functions adjectivally before درفش: literally 'unadorned' (بی- prefix + آراسته). کاویانی is an adjectival/ethnic qualifier ('Kayanian' or 'Kavian') specifying the banner. The phrase describes the banner's appearance; the verb is again elided or implied in narrative style.
segments.s300-g126: همه is a universal quantifier ('all/everyone') preceding پهلوانان ('the champions/heroes'). زرینه is an adjective 'golden' placed before کفش ('shoes/footgear'). The verb ('they had' or 'they wore') is omitted — Persian narrative commonly omits a copula or auxiliary when the state/action is evident from context.
segments.s300-g127: به درد دل is an idiomatic circumstantial phrase meaning 'with pain/sorrow in (their) hearts' (به + noun phrases giving manner). از جای برخاستند is a simple past plural verb phrase: از جای ('from the place') + برخاستند ('they rose/rose up'). The sequence expresses the manner (sorrowful hearts) and the action (they rose).
segments.s300-g128: چپ شاه is a locative/genitive sequence meaning 'the king's left' (چپ = left, followed by the possessor شاه). لشکر is the direct object 'army/host' and بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they arrayed/arranged' (here 'marshalled' or 'set in order'). Word order places the location first, then the object and verb, a normal arrangement in Persian narrative.
segments.s300-g177: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گرسیوز is the proper name (subject). از پشت لشکر is a prepositional phrase: از + پشت (literally 'from/away of the rear') = 'from behind the army'. برفت is the classical past form meaning 'went forth / advanced' (بر- + رفتن); here it narrates a past action.
segments.s300-g178: به پیش برادر is a goal phrase: به + پیش = 'toward/in the presence of (the)'. برادر is the addressee/target 'brother'. خرامید is the past 3rd-sg. of خرامیدن 'to stride/pace proudly'. تفت is an isolated final item here; its lexical senses given (culinary meanings) don't fit the context, so it is likely an archaic particle, vocative, or a corrupted/poetic word — its exact meaning is uncertain.
segments.s300-g179: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'. The first برادر is the subject 'the brother' (narrative actor). روی برادر is a genitive phrase 'the face of the brother' (روی = face). بدید is past 'saw' (3rd-sg.). Note that repetition of برادر marks two participants (subject and the one seen).
segments.s300-g180: به نیرو شد is an idiomatic predicate: به + noun + شدن = 'to become (into) [that noun]' → 'became powerful/filled with force'. شد is the past copula 'became'. و links the next clause. لشکر اندر کشید literally 'drew/withdrew the army inward' — here کشید (past of کشیدن) means 'marshalled/drew together' the troops; اندر is the archaic preposition 'into/within'.
segments.s300-g181: برآمد ز لشکر is 'issued forth / came out from the army' (برآمد = 'arose/issued forth', ز = poetic 'from', لشکر = 'army'). The following sequence 'و دار و گیر' is a simple coordination (X and Y) appended to لشکر; these nouns are listed items drawn from the host. The exact sense of دار and گیر in this passage is obscure in the given lexicon and likely refers to troop-types or equipment; the lexical senses supplied do not match the martial context.
segments.s300-g182: بپوشید is past (3rd person) 'they covered/filled' (ب- prefix + پوشید). روی هوا را is the object phrase 'the surface of the air' = 'the sky' with را marking it definite/object. به تیر is an agent/instrument phrase 'with arrows' (به used to indicate instrument/means). The whole says they covered the sky with arrows.
segments.s300-g183: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید را uses را as an object/topic marker attached to 'the sun'. پشت باریک شد is a compound predicate: پشت 'back/rear' + باریک 'narrow/thin' + شد 'became' — figuratively describing the sun being obscured or reduced (an image for the sun's light diminishing).
segments.s300-g184: ز دیدار شب روز is a causal/temporal phrase 'from / at the meeting (دیدار) of night and day' (ز = 'because/of/at', دیدار = 'meeting/encounter', شب روز = 'night [and] day' presented as coinciding). تاریک شد is 'became dark' (شد = past 'became'), so the clause states that at that meeting the day grew dark.
segments.s300-g65: جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic topical phrase meaning “the whole world” (literally, ‘world head to head’). گفتی is used as a nominal/verbal noun meaning ‘saying/rumor’ (so: ‘it was said’ or ‘people were saying’). از introduces the material or nature (‘of/ made of’), and آهن است is a simple predicate ‘is iron’ (i.e. ‘is made of iron’). Word order: topical subject → noun of saying → complement introduced by از → copula.
segments.s300-g66: وگر is a conditional/concessive ‘if / even if’. آسمان is the subject ‘the sky’; بر here has the sense ‘against’ or ‘toward’ and combines with زمین to mark the object/target (‘against the earth’). دشمن است is the predicate ‘is an enemy’. The clause is concessive: ‘and if the sky is an enemy to the earth…’. Note the classical ordering where the preposition بر precedes its noun.
segments.s300-g67: به هر جای is a locative phrase ‘to/at every place’ (i.e. ‘everywhere’). بر توده places action ‘upon mounds’ (بر = on/upon). چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and the reduplication کوه کوه intensifies the image ‘mountain upon mountain’ or ‘mountainous heaps’. Word order here moves from general location to the vivid simile modifying the heaps.
segments.s300-g68: ز marks origin (‘from’), گردان is ‘troop/squadron’ and governs the following genitive/coordination ایران و توران (‘of Iran and Turan’). گروه is the noun ‘group/troop’ in apposition or summary: ‘groups of squadrons of Iran and Turan’. Classical Persian often places the origin marker z before the noun phrase.
segments.s300-g69: همه modifies the noun phrase that follows (‘all’). ریگ ارمان is a poetic/genitive phrase literally ‘sand of aspiration/longing’ (here meaning every tiny particle, i.e. ‘every speck’). The sequence سر و دست و پای is a standard triad enumerating body parts (‘head and hand and foot’), listing parts to emphasize totality and agitation.
segments.s300-g70: زمین را uses the object/topic marker را to topicalize ‘the earth’ (literally ‘the earth (as for) …’). همی before the verb is a classical auxiliary/particle marking ongoing or emphatic action. دل is the grammatical subject here (‘the heart’), and برآمد (past) means ‘rose’ or ‘was raised’. ز جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from its place’ — together: ‘the heart of the earth rose from its place’ (i.e. the earth trembled).
segments.s300-g71: همه بوم is the subject ‘the whole homeland/land’. شد is the simple past/coprial ‘became/was made’. زیر نعل اندرون is a locative phrase ‘under the horseshoe, within/inside’ (زیر = under; نعل = horseshoe; اندرون = inside). The clause means the entire land lay trampled under horses’ feet; classical order places the verb before the locative adjunct.
segments.s300-g72: چو introduces a comparison ‘as/like/as if’. کرباس آهار داده is a participial phrase ‘sackcloth that has been sized/stiffened’ (آهار داده = ‘given sizing/starching’, a past-participle construction modifying کرباس). به خون attaches the instrument/material ‘with blood’ or ‘stained with blood’. Together the simile reads: ‘as if a coarse cloth had been sized/stiffened with blood’ (i.e. the earth looked like bloody sackcloth).
segments.s300-g33: This is a nominal phrase + past plural verb: 'سپاه دو لشکر' = 'the host / army, two divisions' (a collective + numeral), followed by 'کشیدند' (past 3rd‑person plural) meaning 'they drew up' or 'they formed'. 'صف' is the direct object 'battle line/formation' that was drawn up. Word order here is subject (collective + numeral) → verb → object.
segments.s300-g34: 'همه جنگ را' is an object phrase marked by the object marker 'را' ('all/whole of the war' i.e. the war‑cry or the business of war). The idiom 'بر لب آوردن' means 'to have on the lips / to cry out' and 'آورده' is a past participle/past form 'brought/put (on)'. 'کف' at the end functions as a supplemental noun meaning 'applause/hand‑clapping' (or the sound/act of clapping), so the clause says they put the whole war‑cry on their lips and (raised) clapping. Note 'را' signals that 'جنگ' is the object of 'آورده'.
segments.s300-g35: 'سپهدار ایران' is a title/nominal phrase 'the commander of Iran'. The prepositional/poetic phrase 'ز پشت سپاه' means 'from the back/rear of the army' where 'ز' is the poetic 'from' and 'پشت' = 'rear/behind'; the whole segment localizes the commander relative to the army.
segments.s300-g36: 'بشد' is an archaic/past copular or intransitive past form meaning 'he/it became' or 'he withdrew/it happened that he was'. Here it functions as a past action 'he turned/was made (to be) away'. 'دور' is an adverb/adjective 'away, distant'. 'با' is the preposition 'with' introducing a companion; 'کهتری نیک‌خواه' is a compound in apposition (a proper name or epithet plus 'نیک‌خواه' = 'well‑wisher').
segments.s300-g37: 'چو' is the conjunction 'when'. 'لختی' is a temporal adverb 'for a short while'. 'بیامد' is the past tense 'he came/arrived'. The clause ends with 'پیاده ببود' where 'پیاده' ('on foot' or 'as infantry') is predicative and 'ببود' is an archaic copula/past 'was' — together meaning 'he came on foot / was on foot'.
segments.s300-g38: This is a straightforward transitive clause: 'جهان‌آفرین را' (the Creator of the world, marked by object marker 'را') is the object, 'فراوان' is an adverb 'plentifully/abundantly' modifying the verb, and 'ستود' is the past tense 'praised' (he praised abundantly).
segments.s300-g39: 'بمالید' is the past tense 'he rubbed' (or 'he smote / he pressed'). The object 'رخ را' ('his face', marked by 'را') precedes the verb for emphasis, and the prepositional phrase 'بر آن تیره خاک' ('on that dark earth') gives the surface he rubbed his face against. 'تیره' is an adjective modifying 'خاک'.
segments.s300-g40: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so'. 'گفت' here is the past tense verb 'he said' (note: the listed senses for the lemma in the data are conversation/dialogue, which do not match the verb sense; therefore select -1 for that word). 'کای' is the vocative particle 'O', addressing 'داور' ('Judge/arbiter'); 'داد و پاک' are nouns/adjectival epithets 'justice' and 'pure' used as honorifics following the vocative. The clause is a reported utterance addressed to the divine judge.
segments.s300-g145: دل کوه is a genitive noun phrase (noun+noun) meaning “the heart of the mountain.” گفتی here is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning “said/cried,” and paired with بدرد it reports the mountain’s complaint — بدرد means “it causes pain / aches” (predicate). همی before the verb marks iterative/continuous aspect (kept on ...).
segments.s300-g146: زمین is the subject “the ground/earth.” با سواران is a prepositional phrase “with the horsemen” showing accompaniment. بپرد (from پریدن) means “leaps/jumps” here and, together with همی, conveys a continuing or repeated action (“kept leaping”).
segments.s300-g147: This is a paired, antithetical noun phrase: سر بی تنان = “heads without bodies” (سر + بی + تنان) and تن بی سران = “bodies without heads.” بی is the preposition/particle “without.” The construction lists gruesome battlefield images in inverted order for poetic effect.
segments.s300-g148: چرنگیدن is a verbal form used poetically to mean “to roam/roam about/float about” (here applied to weapons). گرزهای گران is the subject/agent phrase “the heavy maces” (گرزهای = maces, گران = heavy/weighty). Word order places the verb before its noun phrase for poetic cadence.
segments.s300-g149: درخشیدن is a verbal noun or presentive verb meaning “the shining/gleam” (here the action of flashing). It takes خنجر و تیغ تیز as its objects/subjects in a compact, poetic noun phrase: “the gleaming of dagger and sharp sword.” تیغ تیز is noun + adjective (sharp) in usual order.
segments.s300-g150: همی + جست gives an iterative/dramatic past sense (kept leaping / sprang repeatedly). خورشید is the grammatical subject used metaphorically (the sun), and راه گریز is a compound meaning “a way/path of escape.” Together: the sun leapt, seeking an escape route — a hyperbolic image for the dazzling flash.
segments.s300-g151: به دست منوچهر here has an agentive/positional sense “by / in the hand/charge of Manuchehr”; بر میمنه is a set phrase meaning “on the right (side/flank).” The phrase is a locative/positional prepositional cluster describing where someone stood (elliptical verb omitted in the line).
segments.s300-g152: کهیلا is a proper name; the relative که introduces a describing clause. صد شیر بد یک‌تنه is a compact simile: literally “a hundred lions — bad/ferocious — one‑bodied,” i.e., a single man possessing the strength/ferocity of a hundred lions. بد is used figuratively for fierceness, and یک‌تنه emphasizes singleness (one‑bodied / alone) as the locus of that strength.
segments.s300-g113: دو لشکر is the subject (literally 'two hosts') at the start. بر این‌سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus' (بر + demonstrative), and بر آویختند is the verbal core: آویخت- tail (past stem) + -ند (3rd pl.) with the separable prefix بر meaning 'upon/against' so the compound means 'they fell upon / attacked'. Word order is Subject → adverbial → verb.
segments.s300-g114: چنان شد که is a fixed clause 'so/it happened that…' where شد is the past of شدن 'to become/happen'. گفتی here functions as a parenthetical 'as you said' (second-person past of گفتن), but the provided lemma senses list does not include that verbal sense. برآمیختند is past plural (بر‑ + آمیختند) meaning 'they mingled/merged'.
segments.s300-g115: چکاچاک is an onomatopoeic noun describing the sound/flash of metal (here 'clashing/clangor') and functions as the subject. برخاست is past 'rose/was raised'. از هر دو روی is a prepositional phrase 'from each/both sides' (از + هر دو + روی 'side'), so literal structure is 'the clangor rose from both sides.'
segments.s300-g116: ز پرخاش is a causal/ablative phrase 'from/through the attack' (ز = poetic from). خون اندر آمد is the core clause 'blood came in' with اندر = 'into/within' and آمد past 'came'. به جوی marks the destination 'into the stream' (به + جوی). Overall: 'because of the assault, blood came into the stream.'
segments.s300-g117: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. برخاست is past 'rose'. گرد here means 'dust' (an invariable noun) so گرد برخاست = 'dust rose'. از چپ و دست راست is a source phrase 'from the left and the right hand' ('چپ' = left, 'دست راست' = right hand), giving 'when dust rose from left and right.'
segments.s300-g118: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'. خفتان is a participle/adjective 'sleeping' following and qualifying رومی 'the Romans' (postpositive participle). بخواست is past 3rd sg. of خواستن used here in a transitive/causative/intent sense ('sought/attacked/turned against'); context determines whether it's 'desired' or 'made to happen'—here it reads as 'attacked/seized on'.
segments.s300-g119: به یک‌سو is an adverbial phrase 'to one side' (به + یک‌سو). کشیدند is past plural 'they pulled/dragged' (کشید- + -ند). صندوق پیل is a compound object 'the elephant's chest/howdah' (صندوق modifying پیل or vice versa depending on idiom), so the verb takes that as its direct object.
segments.s300-g120: جهان شد is a subject + copular construction 'the world became'. به کردار is a similative/prepositional expression 'in the manner of / like', followed by دریای نیل 'the sea of the Nile' as the comparison; together it means 'the world was turned into/was like the Nile-sea' (a poetic image).
segments.s300-g209: نبد is an archaic/poetic negation (= نبود) meaning 'there was not' or 'did not exist'; کارگر here is adjectival 'effective, working' modifying نیزه; the preposition بر + جوشنش (جوشن + -ش 'his armor') yields 'against/on his cuirass'. Word order (negation + adjective + noun + prepositional phrase) is typical for older Persian.
segments.s300-g210: نه ترس آمد is a simple past tense clause: 'no fear came' (نه + noun + آمد). اندر دل روشنش is a locative phrase 'into/within his روشن دل' where روشنش = روشن + -ش ('his bright/clear'); روشن qualifies دل. The whole clause means 'no fear entered his bright/clear heart.'
segments.s300-g211: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; خسرو is the subject. دل و زور او را is the compound object (دل و زور = 'heart and strength') with او را marking 'his' as definite object (object-marker ra after the pronoun). بدید is the past tense verb 'saw'. The order is 'when Khosrow saw his heart and strength.'
segments.s300-g212: سبک functions adverbially here ('lightly, nimbly'); تیغ تیز is a noun phrase 'sharp sword' (تیغ = sword, تیز = sharp). از میان is a prepositional phrase 'from the middle/from among', and برکشید is the past transitive verb 'drew up/withdrew' (commonly used for drawing a weapon). Together: 'he lightly drew his sharp sword from the midst.'
segments.s300-g213: بزد is simple past 'he struck'; بر میانش = 'upon his middle' (بر + میان + -ش = 'on his midriff'); بدو is an indirect object pronoun here 'at him/to him'; نیم کرد is a causative/compositional phrase 'made half' i.e. 'cut in two'. The sequence of verbs expresses the single combative action: 'he struck him in the middle and cleft him in two.'
segments.s300-g214: دل برز ایلا is a genitival/nominal phrase 'the heart of Barz-Îlā' (برز + ایلا, here a personal/name phrase). پر از بیم کرد uses the idiom 'پر از X کردن' meaning 'to fill (someone) with X'; کرد is causative/past 'made/caused'. So: 'he made Barz-Îlā's heart full of fear.'
segments.s300-g215: سبک here is adjectival/adverbial 'light/nimble' applied to برز ایلا (a named figure): 'the nimble Barz-Îlā'. چو introduces 'when'; آن زخم شاه = 'that wound of the shah'. The clause reads 'when the nimble Barz-Îlā saw that wound of the king.'
segments.s300-g216: بدید is 'he saw' and takes the following coordinated objects: آن دل و زور و آن دستگاه — 'that heart and (that) strength and that apparatus/formation'. The repetition of آن ('that') emphasizes each noun; دستگاه here denotes an organized body/arrangement (the army/formation or apparatus).
segments.s300-g153: جرنجاش is a noun plus the third-person singular possessive suffix -اش (‘his/its’), here meaning 'his vexation/annoyance'. بر is a preposition ('on/upon') connecting to the following noun میسره (an abstract noun here, 'ease/possession' or 'facility'); شد is the past-tense copular/auxiliary 'became', and تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/utterly spoiled'. Together the clause means 'his X became ruined' with an idiomatic result reading 'his vexation came to naught / was ruined'.
segments.s300-g154: به دست is an agentive phrase: به used in the sense 'by' (agent marker) and دست literally 'hand' (here 'by the hand of' = 'by the action of'). The following three words are a name with titles in apposition (فریبرز = proper name; کاووس = Kavus; شاه = king): 'by the hand of Fereyberz, King Kavus.'
segments.s300-g155: یکی is the indefinite article-like word 'a/certain' here; باد و ابری are two coordinated nouns 'a wind and a cloud'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' and نیمروز is the temporal/directional noun 'midday/noon' — 'a wind and a cloud toward midday.'
segments.s300-g156: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/appeared'. رخ 'face/appearance' is the subject noun; هور in classical usage is 'splendor/light' and here personified as the face of light; گیتی فروز is a compound where فروز (an adjective/participle 'bright, illuminating') modifies گیتی 'world' — together 'the face of Hūr, world-illuminating, appeared.'
segments.s300-g157: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said'; که is the subordinating conjunction 'that', introducing the reported clause. ابری is 'a cloud' (indefinite), برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared', and سیاه is the adjective 'black' modifying ابری — 'you said that a black cloud arose.'
segments.s300-g158: ببارید is the past verb 'it rained/fell in drops' used metaphorically here; خون 'blood' is the noun object ('blood/ bloodshed') and اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. آوردگاه is a compound noun 'place of bringing' used as 'battlefield' — 'blood rained on the battlefield.'
segments.s300-g159: بپوشید is the past form of پوشیدن used here intransitively/impersonally 'it covered' or 'it was covered' (classical poetic usage). و simply links clauses 'and'. روی زمین is a noun phrase 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی = surface/face, زمین = earth), and تیره گشت is 'became dark' (تیره adjective 'dark' + گشت 'became').
segments.s300-g160: همی is a classical adverbial particle (here functioning as an intensifier/continuive marker), دیده is 'the eye' (subject), از introduces the cause 'from/by', تیرگی is 'darkness', خیره is an adjective/participial 'stunned/dazed', and گشت is the past 'became' — 'thus the eye became dazed by the darkness.'
segments.s300-g89: ممانید is a prohibitive/polite negative form meaning “do not stay” (addressing the addressee(s)); it appears before its prepositional phrase. بر پیش + noun is a prepositional phrase meaning “in front of / before” (بر + پیش); صندوق و پیل are coordinated objects (“the chest/box and the elephant”). The verb governs the whole phrase: “Do not remain in front of the chest and the elephant.”
segments.s300-g90: سپاهست is the fused classical form سپاه است “(there) is an army / the host is”; it functions as the predicate introducing the state of the forces. بیکار is a predicative adjective “idle / inactive.” بر چند میل is an adverbial phrase: چند is an interrogative/indefinite “how many / how much / what” and میل here means “desire / inclination,” so بر چند میل rhetorically asks “for what desire / why?” — i.e. why do they delay? The clause thus comments on the army’s idle condition.
segments.s300-g91: سوی is the preposition “toward.” میمنه + میسره are the two sides “right (میمنه) and left (میسره)” used in pair; they form the directional object of the verb. برکشید is a past/preterite verb ‘he lifted / drew (up/out)’ (literary), so the clause means “he drew (them) toward right and left.” Note میسره is an archaic/formal word here (the paired counterpart to میمنه).
segments.s300-g92: ز ... و ز is the poetic preposition ز (classical form of از) “from,” repeated for two coordinated sources. قلب and صندوق are the two sources “from the heart / inner chest” and “from the coffer/box.” برتر modifies کشید (here adverbially/intensifying the action) and کشید is the verb ‘drew/pulled’ (literal pulling out). The whole phrase says he pulled out (or raised up) from both the heart and the box.
segments.s300-g93: بفرمود is the honorific/factual past “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result “so that / that,” and here it governs the following proper name and title. جهن is a proper name (Jahan) and رزم آزمای is a compound epithet “battle‑tester / one who tries battle” (رزم = battle, آزمای as a combining element meaning testing/one who tests). Together they form the person ordered to go.
segments.s300-g94: رود here is the verb ‘to go’ (the subjunctive/infinitival form used after تا to express the ordered action: “that he go”). با تگینان لشکر is an instrumental/associative phrase “with the Teginan of the army” (تگینان is a plural/title). ز جای is a classical prepositional phrase “from their place/position” (ز = from, جای = place), indicating they leave their stations.
segments.s300-g95: برد is the past (3rd person singular) form of بردن here meaning 'he brought/led/took' and stands before its object, as is common in classical Persian narrative. دو هزار is the numeral phrase 'two thousand' quantifying سوار; آزموده is a past‑participle adjective ('tested/experienced') used adjectivally with سوار to yield 'seasoned horsemen'.
segments.s300-g96: همه is a distributive/quantifying word 'all' modifying نیزه‌دار ('spear‑bearers' or 'spearmen'). از introduces a source/directional phrase 'from/out of'; در here is the noun 'gate/door' (درِ کارزار = 'the gate of the battle') with کارزار ('battle/field') in a genitive relationship. In classical poetic style the ezāfe may be unmarked, so از در کارزار functions adverbially to indicate they came out from the battle‑gate into the fight.
segments.s300-g201: ندیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural negated verb ‘did not see’ (verb after its object is normal in Persian poetic word order). The following noun phrase گرسیوز و جهن روی is the thing not seen: two proper names joined by و. جهن روی functions as a compound name in which روی (‘face’) is part of the epithet/name rather than a separate descriptive noun.
segments.s300-g202: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that’/complementizer) giving what was not seen. او is the 3rd-person singular pronoun ‘he’. پیش خسرو is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of Khosrow’ (پیش = in front of; خسرو = Khosrow). شود is the classical present/subjunctive form of شدن ‘become/come to be’ and here expresses the eventuality (‘would appear / become’). رزمجوی is a predicate noun ‘war-seeker / fighter’ completing the clause: ‘that he would appear/become a warrior before Khosrow.’ Word order: pronoun + locative phrase + verb + predicate noun.
segments.s300-g203: عنانش = عنان (reins) + enclitic ـش ‘his’ (object/possessive). گرفتند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they seized/took’. و links the next action. بر is a directional/prepositional particle here meaning ‘toward/on’ and تافتند is a poetic past 3rd plural of تافتن meaning ‘turned’/’bent (their horses)’ — together بر تافتند = ‘they turned toward’. The clause describes a quick, physical action (grabbing reins and turning).
segments.s300-g204: سوی is a preposition ‘toward(s)’. ریگ آموی is a place-phrase ‘the sands of the Amu’ (ریگ ‘sand’ + آموی, here indicating the Amu/Amu River—a toponymic genitive); the whole is the directional object. بشتافتند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they hurried/rushed’. Word order: preposition + place phrase + verb.
segments.s300-g205: چون introduces a temporal clause ‘when/as’. او بازگشت is ‘he returned’ (بازگشت here functioning as the verb ‘returned’). استقیلا is an adverb ‘independently / alone’ modifying the return. چو گرد is a brief, poetic adverbial phrase: چو (‘as/when’) + گرد (‘about/around’) giving the sense ‘as he came around/approached’ — it sets up the coming action in the next segment. Overall: ‘When he returned alone and (as) came around...’
segments.s300-g206: بیامد is past 3rd-singular ‘he came’. که introduces the content/purpose clause that follows: با شاه ‘with the king’ (prepositional phrase) precedes the verb جوید (classical present/subjunctive-like form of جویدن ‘to seek’) and نبرد is the object ‘battle’. The clause expresses purpose/intention: ‘he came in order that he might seek battle with the king.’ Note the classical use of جوید (present-form) after a past narrative verb to express intent.
segments.s300-g207: دمان here means ‘hem/flank/foothill’ (a physical edge/side) and is linked to شاه ایلا as a genitive/possessive unit ‘the flank (or skirt) of King Īlā’ (poetic word order places noun before modifiers). به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase ‘in front of/the presence of the army’ (به پیش = before/in front of; سپاه = the host/army). The whole gives the location (by the king’s flank, before the army).
segments.s300-g208: یکی is an indefinite pronoun ‘someone / a certain one’ serving as the subject. نیزه is the instrument/object ‘a spear’. زد is past 3rd-singular of زدن ‘struck/thrust’. بر marks the target ‘on/at’ and کمرگاه شاه is the target noun phrase ‘the king’s waist/small of the back’. Word order here is typical: indefinite subject + instrument + verb + prepositional target.
segments.s300-g17: بپیوست is a past 3rd-person singular verb ('joined/began'); here it introduces an action meaning 'a battle began' with جنگی as the event noun ('a battle'). کزان is the relative/prepositional particle 'from which/of which' and سان is an adverbial particle 'such/so', together forming a modifier for نشان ('a sign'); the whole fragment reads roughly 'a battle began, of which such a sign...'.
segments.s300-g18: ندادند is the past negative plural ('they did not give'); the subject follows the verb in postverbal position for poetic emphasis: گردان ('troop/squadron') with گردنکشان as an epithet/appositive ('the neck‑breakers' = rebellious/stubborn ones). Together: 'the rebellious squadrons gave no sign.'
segments.s300-g19: بکشتند is past plural 'they killed.' چندان here is a quantifier ('so many/to that extent'), and ز + توران سپاه is a poetic genitive/prepositional phrase 'of Turan's army' (ز = 'from/of'). The clause says 'they killed so many of Turan's host.'
segments.s300-g20: که introduces the result/relative clause ('so that' / 'such that'), دریای خون is a nominal phrase 'a sea of blood', and گشت is past 3rd-singular 'became/turned (into)'; آوردگاه is the predicate noun 'the battlefield'. The clause means 'so that the battlefield became a sea of blood.'
segments.s300-g21: چنین بود is a fixed expression 'it was thus.' تا here is temporal 'until', introducing the time clause آسمان تیره گشت ('the sky became dark'), where تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous' and گشت is past 'became.'
segments.s300-g22: همان functions as an intensifier/pointing word 'the very' or 'likewise'; چشم جنگاوران is a genitive construction 'the eyes of the warriors' (noun + possessor), and خیره گشت is a compound predicate 'became fixed/staring' (خیره = 'staring/astonished', گشت = 'became').
segments.s300-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' پیروز شد is a past construction 'became victorious.' In this poetic order the subject and its epithet follow the verb: قارن رزم زن = 'Qaran, the battle‑striker/warrior' (رزم + زن = one who strikes in battle), so the clause reads 'when Qaran the warrior became victorious.'
segments.s300-g24: به ... اندر آمد is a poetic locative motion 'came into / entered to.' جهن is a proper name (place or person) governed by به, دلیر is an adjective 'brave' describing the subject, and شکن is the subject noun 'breaker' (likely a personal epithet/name). Word order is inverted: destination (به جهن) + epithet (دلیر) + verb (اندر آمد) + subject (شکن).
segments.s300-g41: The clause begins with the subject pronoun تو + present-tense verb دانی 'you know'. کز here is an archaic fused particle (که + ز) meaning 'that/from' and introduces the reason/agent: 'at his hands'. من is the emphatic subject and دیده‌ام is a perfect participle construction ('I have seen/experienced'), so ستم دیده‌ام idiomatically means 'I have suffered injustice.'
segments.s300-g42: بسی functions as a quantifier/adverb 'many' or 'much' modifying روز; the adjective بد follows the noun (typical Persian order: noun + adjective). را is the definite object marker attaching to 'روز بد', and پسندیده‌ام is a perfect form 'I have chosen/approved' — here used ironically or idiomatically to mean 'I have endured/chosen many bad days.'
segments.s300-g43: مکافات (retribution) is used as the object/command nominal and is followed by the light verb کن (imperative 'do!'). بدکنش is the target 'the evildoer', marked by را as definite object; به خون gives the means/manner 'with blood' (به + noun indicating 'by/with' here). The whole phrase is an imperative: 'do retribution, (shed) the evildoer's blood.'
segments.s300-g44: باشی is an optative/imperative form of بودن ('be, may you be'), so تو باشی functions as an injunction 'you be...'. ستم دیده is a noun phrase formed with a past participle 'seen/oppressed' and, with را as topic/object marker, is what you are to guide; رهنمون is the predicate noun 'guide/guidance' following the verb. Word order places the complement after باشی for emphasis: 'you be the guide of the oppressed.'
segments.s300-g45: وز here is an archaic/conjunctive prepositional form (classical 'و ز' = 'and from/from that') introducing origin or motion; because the supplied sense list doesn't include this use it's marked below as unknown. آن جایگه = 'that place/position'. با دلی 'with a heart' uses با + noun; دلی پر ز غم is a small clause 'a heart full (پر) of grief' where ز is the classical preposition 'of/from' (introducing the content causing fullness).
segments.s300-g46: پر از کین is a repeated 'full of' construction (پر + از + noun) meaning 'full of vengeance'. The following phrase سر از تخمه زادشم is an appositive/nominal phrase: سر here means 'chief/leader' (head), از is the preposition 'from', تخمه 'seed/stock' means 'lineage/offspring', and زادشم is a proper name — together 'a chief from the stock of Zādsham.'
segments.s300-g47: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. خروشان is an adverbial participle/adjective 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the manner. به قلب سپاه uses به + noun phrase to indicate motion/direction 'into the heart/core of the army/host.'
segments.s300-g48: This is a verbal phrase with the verb نهاد (past of نهادن 'placed/put') meaning 'he placed'. به سر بر نهاد is the common collocation 'placed upon the head' where به + سر marks the target and بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. آن خجسته کلاه is the definite object 'that auspicious cap' placed on the head.
segments.s300-g81: برفتند is a third-person plural past verb (they went/advanced); here it precedes the following noun phrase by poetic inversion. گردان نیزه‌وران is a noun phrase 'a troop (گردان) of spearmen (نیزه‌وران)' with the head گردان followed by a descriptive noun; the whole segment reads 'a troop of spearmen went forth.'
segments.s300-g82: هم از introduces an additive prepositional phrase 'also/from' (هم = also; از = from). قلب لشکر is a genitive construction 'the heart/core of the army' (قلب = core/heart; لشکر = host/army). سپاهی گران is a postpositive descriptive phrase where سپاهی ('soldiers/warriors') is qualified by گران ('heavy, mighty'), yielding 'the heavy-armed host.' The whole gives 'also from the heart of the heavy host.'
segments.s300-g83: نگه کرد is a compound verb formed from the noun نگه ('a look, a gaze') plus the light verb کرد ('did/made'), functioning as 'he looked' in the past. افراسیاب is the subject placed between the compound parts; از دو میل is a prepositional phrase 'from two masts/pillars' giving the vantage point of the look (میل here = mast/pillar).
segments.s300-g84: بدان is the contracted/poetic 'به آن' ('toward that' / 'to that'); here it signals direction/target of the look. لشکر و جنگ links two related nouns 'army and battle' (جنگ meaning the fight/battle) and صندوق و پیل are apposed nouns specifying the subject of the battle ('the chest and the elephant'). The sequence is therefore 'toward that army and the battle — the chest and the elephant.'
segments.s300-g85: همه quantifies the following coordinated objects. ژنده modifies پیلان (a poetic/epic adjective 'ragged' or 'tattered' applied to elephants), so 'all the ragged elephants and the army'. براند is a past verb 'drove away' (third person) that closes the clause: 'he drove away all the ...'. Word order places the verb at the end as typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s300-g86: جهان تیره شد is a simple predicative clause 'the world became dark' with شد as the past-tense copula/verb 'became.' روشنایی نماند is a negative-result clause 'no light remained' where نماند is the past negative of مانَد (to remain): 'there remained no light.' The two clauses are sequential result: darkness came and light was gone.
segments.s300-g87: خروشید is an intransitive verb 'he cried/roared' (there was a cry). It introduces the direct address that follows: کای is the vocative particle 'O' and نامداران جنگ is the vocative phrase 'renowned men of war/battle' (نامداران = famed ones; جنگ = battle/war used as the field/qualification). The verb + vocative order is a poetic way to render a loud exclamation directed at the warriors.
segments.s300-g88: چه دارید is a rhetorical question 'what do you have / what is it you are doing' with داريد present-tense plural 'you have.' بر خویش تن is a prepositional phrase 'upon your own bodies' formed from بر + خویش (here written as خوی + ش, where ش is the possessive suffix '= his/her/your') + تن 'body.' جای تنگ is a noun phrase 'a narrow place' (جای = place; تنگ = narrow) describing the confinement — so the line asks why they keep/hold their bodies in a cramped place.
segments.s300-g185: فریبنده is an adjectival epithet placed before the name to qualify it ('deceitful'/'treacherous'). گرسیوز is the proper name. پهلوان is a title/appositive 'champion, hero' that follows the name; the whole string reads as 'the deceitful Garsivaz, the champion.'
segments.s300-g186: بیامد is a simple past 3rd-person verb 'came.' به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of' marking the direction of coming. برادر نوان is a noun (برادر 'brother') followed by the adjective نوان ('young, youthful') in post-nominal position (poetic/older word order), so the phrase means 'his/ the young brother.'
segments.s300-g187: The initial که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). اکنون 'now' sets the time. ز گردان literally 'from the troop(s)' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register). The second که introduces a relative clause modifying گردان: جوید is a present/habitual 3rd-person verb 'seeks/desires' and نبرد is its object 'battle'; together 'the troop that seeks battle.'
segments.s300-g188: زمین 'the earth' followed by پر ز خون is a poetic pattern meaning 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', خون = 'blood' or 'bloodshed'). The parallel phrase آسمان پر ز گرد uses the same structure to mean 'the sky full of dust.' This is a concise, poetic parallelism.
segments.s300-g189: سپه بازکش is an imperative instruction addressed to a commander: سپه 'the army' plus بازکش, an imperative formed from باز + کشیدن meaning 'pull back/bring back/withdraw' (imperative). چون شب آمد is a temporal clause 'when night comes' (چون here = 'when/as'), and مکوش is a negative imperative 'do not strive/attempt.' Together: 'Withdraw the army; when night comes, do not attempt (to continue the fight).'
segments.s300-g190: که again introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that.' اکنون 'now' sets immediacy. برآید is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'will arise/appear.' ز ترکان marks the source 'from the Turks' (ز = 'from/of', ترکان = 'the Turks'). خروش is the noun 'uproar/tumult/cry.' The clause says 'for now an uproar will arise from the Turks.'
segments.s300-g191: تو در جنگ باشی is a direct imperative/advice: تو 'you' + در جنگ 'in battle' + باشی (subjunctive/imperative form of بودن) 'be/remain' = 'you remain in the fight.' سپه در گریز is a nominal phrase 'the army in flight/retreat'; the negative verb (مکن) that follows applies to this phrase ('do not let the army be in flight').
segments.s300-g192: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not.' با تن خویش is an instrumental phrase 'with your own body/person' (با = 'with/by', تن = 'body/person', خویش represented here as خوی + ش = 'own/his/her' — a reflexive/possessive), meaning 'at the cost of yourself.' چندین ستیز 'many a combat/strife' is the object: چندین 'several/many a' and ستیز 'strife/contest.' The whole: 'Do not, with your own person, undertake many fights.'
segments.s300-g137: همه (determiner) precedes the noun to mark totality ('all/the whole'). ریگ is the head noun ('sand'). پر here is a postpositive adjective/participial adjective meaning 'full (of)' and takes the following nouns as its complement (i.e. 'full of X'). خسته و کشته are coordinated nouns/adjectives describing those who fill the sand. بود is the past copula placed at the end to assert the state ('was').
segments.s300-g138: This clause states that nobody had turned back that day. کسی را uses را as an emphatic/topic marker ('not a single person'). کجا here functions rhetorically (classical usage) to imply 'ever/anywhere' (intensifying the negation), coupled with روز 'that day/time'. برگشته is the past participle/perfective 'returned', and بود is the past copula, yielding a sense of 'had returned' or 'was returned' in past context.
segments.s300-g139: ز introduces a causal or originative relation ('from/because of'). بس here means an abundance/so many (classical use: 'so much/so many'), governing کشته 'the slain'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and دشت 'plain' names the location; آوردگاه is a compound noun 'battlefield' (lit. 'place of bringing' but used here as 'place of battle').
segments.s300-g140: همی is an archaic/epic particle marking continuous or iterative action ('kept on/continually'). راندند is the past 3rd-person-plural of راندن ('they drove/rode'). The object/affected thing اسب ('horses') follows; بر + کشته گاه forms a prepositional phrase 'over/above the place of the slain' — گاه here is used in the compound sense 'place' (classical usage), and the compound denotes the spot where the slain lie.
segments.s300-g141: This is a simile: بیابان is the topic 'the desert'. به کردار literally 'in the manner/appearance' introduces the simile 'like Jihun' (جیحون = the Amu Darya). ز خون ('from/of blood') gives the reason or materializing image (the desert looked as if made of blood). Word order places the topic first and the descriptive simile and cause after it.
segments.s300-g142: A paired nominal phrase giving two states: یکی ... و دیگری ... ('one ... and another ...'). Each adjective-like compound (بی‌سر 'headless', سرنگون 'overthrown') follows its numeral/demonstrative; the copula is omitted because this is continuing description of the desert's condition from the previous clause.
segments.s300-g143: خروش is the noun 'roar/uproar' serving as the event/topic. سواران و اسبان are coordinated animate sources ('riders and horses'). ز دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain' marking origin. Classical Persian often places the source phrase after the coordinated nouns as here.
segments.s300-g144: ز introduces cause/origin ('from/by'). بانگ is 'the cry/shout' and تبیره (an uncommon/poetic word here) functions as the content of that cry (likely a battle-cry or shouted word); together 'the cry "tabireh"'. همی again marks continuous/iterative action, and برگذشت is the past of گذشتن with the verbal prefix بر- indicating 'passed by/moved past'.
segments.s300-g57: This is a short predicative clause in which the adjective سیه ('black') precedes the past verb شد ('became') — a normal poetic order for 'was blackened'. ز گرد سپاه is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from the dust of the host' that precedes the subject آفتاب (sun) for emphasis; in ordinary prose you would expect آفتاب از گرد سپاه سیه شد. شد is a simple past intransitive 'became'.
segments.s300-g58: ز here introduces another cause/source phrase 'from' (ز + noun) and links back to the previous clause; the phrase is elliptical (no verb) and supplies additional things that blackened or filled the air. پیکان الماس is a poetic apposition/genitive (→ 'arrows (of) diamond' or 'diamond-headed arrows') where the ezāfe is omitted in classical style; پر عقاب is literally 'feather of the eagle'.
segments.s300-g59: ز بس is an idiomatic causal/quantitative opening 'from so many / because of so much' (here introducing the abundant sounds/effects). ناله بوق is a genitive/construct ('the wailing of the trumpet') with the ezāfe omitted poetically. گرد سپاه likewise is 'the dust of the host'; the whole segment is verbless and descriptive, continuing the scene.
segments.s300-g60: Another causal/locative phrase introduced by ز linking to the previous noises: بانگ سواران is 'the cry/shout of the riders' (genitive, ezāfe omitted). در آن رزمگاه is a straightforward locative 'in that battlefield'. The segment is an adjunct, not a separate finite clause.
segments.s300-g61: همی is an archaic/classical adverb here marking ongoing or emphatic action ('all at once / presently / indeed'); آب گشت آهن is a simple past construction 'the water became iron' (گشت = became). The coordinated و کوه و سنگ extends the predicate elliptically: 'and the mountain and the stone (also [became like iron])'.
segments.s300-g62: This is a parallel locative nominative phrase with an omitted copula: به دریا نهنگ و به هامون پلنگ = 'in the sea (was) a whale and in the plain (was) a panther.' به marks the location/setting (here 'into/in') and the small clauses are verbless, a common poetic device.
segments.s300-g63: This is a pair of adjectival phrases: زمین پر ز جوش = 'the earth, full from/with seething' and هوا پر خروش = 'the air, full of tumult'. پر is used as 'full (of)' and ز introduces the source ('of/with'); the second member omits ز by ellipsis, a common poetic compression.
segments.s300-g64: A simple past transitive clause: هژبر is the subject (a proper name/epithet), ژیان را uses را to mark the affected group 'the living' as definite/affected, بدرید is the past 'tore', and گوش is the thing torn 'the ear(s)'. Word order is poetic but follows S + O (with را) + V + object-complement pattern to present the violent action.
segments.s300-g129: 'سوی راستش رستم کینه جوی' is a preposed locative + nominal phrase. 'سوی راستش' (= 'on/to his right') sets the direction/position; it is followed by the name 'رستم' in apposition and the compound epithet 'کینه جوی' ('hatred‑seeking', i.e. 'vengeful') which modifies Rostam. Word order is natural for classical Persian: directional phrase -> name -> epithet.
segments.s300-g130: 'زواره برادرش بنهاد روی' contains the target + possessive + verb phrase. 'زواره' is a proper name here (the person toward whom the action is directed), 'برادرِ ش' = 'his brother' (ش is the enclitic possessive), and 'بنهاد روی' is the past preterite verb 'he placed/put' or 'he turned' + 'روی' 'face' — together meaning 'he turned his face toward Zowareh, his brother.' The verb 'بنهاد' is third‑person past and governs the object/directional complement 'روی'.
segments.s300-g131: 'جهاندیده گودرز کشوادگان' is a nominal cluster listing persons. 'جهاندیده' is an adjective 'worldly‑wise' placed before the proper name 'گودرز' (an epithet + name). 'کشوادگان' is an archaic/uncertain collective noun following the name (appositive or epithet); its exact meaning is specialized/archaic, so it behaves as a noun in the same coordinate list.
segments.s300-g132: 'بزرگان بسیار و آزادگان' continues the list: 'بزرگان' (plural noun 'great ones' or nobles) followed by the quantifier 'بسیار' (post‑nominal here, functioning like 'many'), then the conjunction 'و' and 'آزادگان' ('the freemen'). This is a straightforward noun phrase enumerating social groups.
segments.s300-g133: 'ببودند بر دست رستم به پای' is a verbal phrase followed by two locative/possessive phrases. 'ببودند' is the past plural 'they were'; 'بر دست رستم' literally 'on the hand of Rostam' idiomatically means 'in Rostam's power/control'; 'به پای' ('at the feet of') introduces the person(s) to whom the following names refer. The clause sets the state (they were under his control) and links to the named figures in the next segment.
segments.s300-g134: 'زرسب و منوشان فرخنده رای' names the persons: two names joined by 'و' with a following adjectival phrase 'فرخنده رای' ('of auspicious/fortunate counsel') that modifies (most naturally) the preceding name(s). In Persian names and epithets, the adjective ('فرخنده') + noun ('رای') form a compound epithet 'of auspicious counsel' describing Manushan (and by extension the pair).
segments.s300-g135: 'برآمد ز آوردگاه گیر و دار' is a clause headed by the perfect/past 'برآمد' ('arose/issued forth') with the source 'ز آوردگاه' ('from the battlefield'). 'گیر و دار' is an idiomatic frozen pair meaning the grip/hold — here 'the mêlée/the thick of the fight' — functioning as the subject or the event that arose.
segments.s300-g136: 'ندیدند ز آنگونه کس کارزار' is a negative past clause: 'ندیدند' = 'they did not see'; 'ز آنگونه' = 'of/such as that' (qualifier); 'کس' = 'any person/one'; 'کارزار' = 'battle/struggle'. The whole unit states that they had never seen a battle by any man like that — 'کس' is an indefinite/general 'person' here.
segments.s300-g225: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. آوردگه is a compound noun 'place/venue' with the following adjective خوار 'despised/abasing' modifying it (word order: noun + adjective). بگذاشتند is past-tense plural 'they left/abandoned' (classical b- prefix + گذاشتند), so the whole segment means 'when they abandoned/left the humiliating place.'
segments.s300-g226: بفرمود is an honorific/periphrastic past 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that/whereupon') linking the command to what follows. بانگ is 'a cry/clamor' and برداشتند is past plural 'they took/raised' (here 'raise/utter a cry'). The clause thus reads 'he commanded, and so they raised a cry.'
segments.s300-g227: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). این شیر مردی is a nominal phrase 'this lion (i.e. lionlike man/warrior)' (شیر = 'lion', مردی = 'man/warrior'). ز + زنگ شب is a prepositional phrase 'from/like the bell/alarm of the night' (ز here = poetic 'from' or 'of'). است is the copula 'is', so the clause states a characterization: 'this lionly man is an alarm of the night.'
segments.s300-g228: The sequence 'م' + 'را' should be read together as مرا ('for me / to me'); باز گشتن is the infinitive 'to return' (باز = 'again', گشتن = 'to turn/return'). ز تنگ شب is a prepositional phrase 'from the night's narrowness/straits' and the final است is the copula. Grammatically this gives a dative-infinitive construction: 'for me, to return from the night's narrow straits is ...' (the clause states this as a fact or necessity).
segments.s300-g229: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. ایدون is an optative/preterite-form meaning 'may it come/occur' or 'it should happen'. که introduces the subordinate content, امروز یکبار 'today once' gives the temporal limit, and باد at the end functions as an optative particle 'may it be' (classical use). The whole unit is an optative conditional: 'If it should happen that today, even once, may it be so…'.
segments.s300-g230: ترا (here shown split as ت + را) is the 2nd-person object 'you'. جست is a past form used as optative 'may it seek' or 'may it come upon' (sense here = 'seek'); و is the conjunction 'and'. شادی ترا (again ت + را) is 'joy to you' and در گشاد literally 'in opening/wide' — idiomatically 'may joy be opened/let joy be made wide for you.' The optative particle باد in the previous segment scopes over these wishes, so the segment expresses two wishes: 'may it seek you, and may joy open to you.'
segments.s300-g231: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. روشن کند is a verbal phrase 'makes bright/illuminates' (کند = 'does/makes' with روشن as object/adverbial), and روز روی زمین is the direct object phrase 'the day (makes) the face of the earth' (روز = day, روی = face/surface, زمین = earth). Word order follows classical Persian subordinate clause pattern with the verb before its object and the copula omitted.
segments.s300-g232: درفش دل‌افروز is a noun phrase 'the banner/standard that cheers the heart' (درفش = 'standard', دل‌افروز = 'heart‑delighting/cheering'). ما را is a possessive/object phrase 'our/for us' (ما + را = 'our'), placed before the verb for emphasis. ببین is the imperative 'see/behold' (or a hortative 'you will see' in narrative use); together it commands the addressee to 'behold our cheering banner.'
segments.s300-g49: خروش آمد و ناله گاودم is a coordinate clause: خروش آمد ‘a roar came/rose’ (خروش subject, آمد simple past), followed by و linking the next noun phrase ناله گاودم ‘the moan/lowing of the ox (گاودم)’. گاودم is a genitive modifier of ناله (the possessive relation is implicit: ‘the moan of the ox’). Word order is N V (subject+verb) then conjunction + N (possessive noun phrase).
segments.s300-g50: دم نای رویین و رویینه خم is a nominal phrase describing sounds/parts: دم نای ‘the breath/blast of the ney (reed‑flute)’ where دم is an instrument‑related noun (breath) governed by نای. Then two coordinated modifiers رویین و رویینه modify خم: both adjectives indicate hardness/iron quality (iron-faced, iron‑made) and خم ‘curve/bend’ names the curved part (likely of an instrument or bow). The conjunction و links the two adjectival forms; overall the phrase strings together sound + instrument image.
segments.s300-g51: وز آن روی لشکر به کردار کوه starts with وز used poetically as a prepositional/relational particle ('from/with/regarding that…'); آن روی ‘that face/aspect’ is the noun phrase governing لشکر ‘the army’. به کردار کوه is a simile phrase ‘in the manner/shape of a mountain’ (به + abstract noun کردار ‘manner/behaviour’ + simile noun کوه). Word order places the cause/ground (وز آن روی) before the subject phrase لشکر and then the simile phrase.
segments.s300-g52: برفتند جوشان گروها گروه is the main verb clause: برفتند (3rd‑pl. past) ‘they went/advanced’ followed by the participial/ adjectival جوشان ‘seething/boiling’ describing their movement. گروها گروه is a tautological poetic piling of two nouns meaning ‘groups, group’, effectively ‘band after band’ or ‘group upon group’ and functions adverbially to indicate how they advanced. The verb precedes these adverbial phrases as normal.
segments.s300-g53: سپاهی به کردار دریای آب is a noun phrase: سپاهی ‘a host/army’ modified by the simile به کردار دریای آب ‘in the fashion of a sea of water’ (به + کردن/کردار introducing the manner + دریای آب ‘sea of water’). The simile phrase follows the head noun, as commonly done in Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s300-g54: به قلب اندرون جهن و افراسیاب is a prepositional/locative phrase giving the direction/target: به ‘to/towards’ + قلب اندرون ‘the inner heart/centre’ (قلب modified by اندرون ‘inside’). The following nouns جهن و افراسیاب are proper names serving as the locus/objects of that motion (‘into Jahan and Afrasiab’). The sequence places the destination phrase after the directional preposition as expected.
segments.s300-g55: چو هر دو سپاه اندر آمد ز جای is a temporal clause introduced by چو ‘when’. هر دو سپاه literally ‘each two hosts’ here idiomatically means ‘both hosts’; اندر آمد is the past compound ‘came in/entered’ (اندر an older prepositional/locative element + آمد past verb), and ز جای ‘from their places/positions’ marks the source with poetic ز ‘from’. Word order is subordinating particle + subject phrase + verb + source phrase.
segments.s300-g56: تو گفتی که دارد در و دشت پای is a reported clause: تو گفتی ‘you said’ (second‑person past) followed by the complementizer که ‘that’ and the present form دارد ‘it has/there is’. در و دشت پای is a conjoined noun phrase ‘door/gate and plain’s foot/footing’ where در is ‘gate/entrance’ and پای ‘foot/base’ qualifies دشت ‘plain’; grammatically دارد governs this compound as its logical complement. The overall structure is reporting speech with a that‑clause in which دارد takes a nominal predicate.
segments.s300-g73: وز آن پس here forms a temporal phrase 'after that' (وز is an archaic/prepositional element; in classical Persian the sequence آن پس = 'that, then/after'). دلیران افراسیاب is a noun phrase 'the brave ones of Afrasiab' with the modifier (the name افراسیاب) following the plural noun دلیران, common in poetic/older Persian (the ezāfe linking is omitted in verse).
segments.s300-g74: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went / departed'. بر سان is a comparative locative: بر + سان = 'upon/like', introducing the simile that follows; کشتی is the noun 'ship' and بر آب repeats the locative preposition 'on the water' (the doubled بر is a poetic device to mark two locative phrases). Word order (verb then comparative phrase then locative) reflects Persian poetry's flexible ordering.
segments.s300-g75: به here marks the direction/placement 'onto' (they placed onto); صندوق پیلان 'boxes of the elephants' (پیلان is the genitive/plural form: 'elephants' boxes' — i.e. howdahs). نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/put', and روی functions adverbially as 'on / upon' indicating the placement on top of the elephants. Note the object phrase comes before the verb, a common order in Persian.
segments.s300-g76: کجا here is a relative/adverbial 'where' (introducing a subordinate location clause). ناوک‌انداز is a compound noun 'arrow‑thrower' (archer). بود is the past copula 'was', and اندر اوی means 'inside it/him' (اندر = 'in/within', اوی = old 3rd‑person pronoun 'it/him/her'). The whole clause: 'where an archer was inside it.'
segments.s300-g77: حصاری is an indefinite/attributive noun 'a fortress / enclosure'. بد here is an evaluative adjective 'bad/terrible' used poetically to mean 'formidable' or 'fierce'. از پیل means 'of/from elephants' (material or composition). پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the army' — note the preposition follows the noun phrase as is normal in Persian.
segments.s300-g78: برآورده is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'raised/erected' (from برآوردن / برآوردن in poetic usage). بر قلب 'on/at the heart' uses بر as a locative 'upon' with قلب meaning 'core/centre'. و links the next phrase; بر بسته راه = 'upon (and) closed the road' — بسته is adjective 'closed', راه 'road/way'. The sequence describes position (at the center) and effect (blocking the way).
segments.s300-g79: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. صندوق پیلان 'the elephants' boxes/chests' (source of the arrows). ببارید is an intransitive past-form verb 'it rained / they rained down' — used here with تیر (arrow) as the thing that rained. Note the verb precedes the noun تیر (verb–subject), a word order common in Persian poetry.
segments.s300-g80: برآمد is 'arose/issued forth' (past). خروشیدن is the infinitival/verbal noun 'to roar / a roar' so the phrase means 'a roar arose' or 'there was an outcry'. دار و گیر is a paired nominal phrase; دار can mean 'abode/tree/etc.' and گیر 'seizure/hold', but here the pair functions idiomatically to signal commotion/uproar and struggle — a condensed poetic merism following the verb. Grammatically the verb governs this verbal noun phrase and the linked nouns by و form a composite description of the sound/effect.
segments.s300-g217: This is a short clause with the locative preposition first: 'به تاریکی اندر' = 'into the darkness' (به + noun + اندر, an emphatic/poetic double locative). 'گریزان' is a present‑participle/adjective meaning 'fleeing' that modifies the subject; 'برفت' is the past tense verb 'went off/left' placed at the end in typical Persian word order.
segments.s300-g218: 'همی' here is an adverb giving an emphatic/limiting sense ('just/merely' or 'indeed') before the noun phrase. 'پوست بر تنش' = 'the skin on his body' (بر + تن + enclitic pronoun ش = 'his'); the sequence 'گفتی بکفت' is a poetic idiom: بکفت = 'was enough', and گفتی functions as a connexive/colloquial/poetic particle linked to بکفت (note: گفتی in the provided lexicon is mis‑annotated as 'conversation').
segments.s300-g219: Subject 'سپه' (the army) is followed by the temporal/subordinate marker 'چون' + past verb 'بدیدند' = 'when they saw'. 'ز او دستبرد' is a genitive/source phrase with ز = 'from/by' indicating the attack came from him; 'دستبرد' here means an armed seizure/assault rather than simple 'theft'.
segments.s300-g220: 'به آوردگه' = 'at the place/assembly (court)' with به marking location. The verb phrase is negative: 'بر نماند' = 'did not remain/was not left' (بر + نماند), followed by 'ایچ گرد' ('not a thing/none at all' — a negative intensifier); word order places the locative first, verb toward the end, and the negative quantifier after the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s300-g221: 'بر افراسیاب' = 'to/for Afrasiab' (بر marking the direction/target of an effect). 'آن سخن مرگ بود' literal order 'that word death was' — a verbless past copula 'بود' states equivalence: 'that speech was death.' The construction is typical of classical Persian: topic + predicate + بود.
segments.s300-g222: This is a relative/temporal clause introduced by 'کجا' here functioning like 'for/where/when' in older poetic usage. 'پشت خود را' = 'his back' with را marking the object/topic; 'بدیشان' = 'to them'; 'نمود' is the past tense of 'نمودن' meaning 'showed/turned (one's back)'. Word order: adverbial (کجا) + object + indirect object + verb.
