segments.s300-g223: 'ز تورانیان' = 'from/by the Turanians' with ز as a poetic genitive/source marker. 'او چو آگاه شد' = 'when he became aware/informed' where چو = 'when' (classical/poetic use) and شد is the perfective/past auxiliary 'became'. The clause thus gives the time/trigger for the reporting clause that follows.
segments.s300-g224: The main reporting clause begins with the vocative/subject 'تو' ('you'). 'گفتی' in the provided lexicon is mis‑annotated; here it functions as the past/perfective verb 'said' (or an impersonal/reporting particle in some readings) and introduces the reported content 'بر او روز کوتاه شد' = 'his day became short' (بر او = 'for him', روز کوتاه شد = 'the day was shortened/ his time was short').
segments.s300-g169: نگه کرد is the past tense of the light-verb construction نگه‌داشتن/نگه‌کردن 'to look/gaze'; نگه (root noun/verb-stem) + کرد (past of کردن) yields 'looked.' The proper name گرسیوز follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian), and the prepositional phrase از پشت شاه 'from behind the king' modifies where he looked or from where he acted.
segments.s300-g170: به جنگ اندر آورد is a compact, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'brought/led into battle': به + جنگ (into/for battle) + اندر (archaic 'in/into') + آورد (past of آوردن 'to bring'). یکسر سپاه follows as the direct object (modifier + noun): یکسر 'entire/whole' qualifies سپاه 'army.' The verb آورد governs the object placed after it, yielding 'brought the whole army into battle.'
segments.s300-g171: سپاهی here names the force/contingent being sent and functions as the object of فرستاد (past 'sent'). The prepositional phrase بر میمنه ('to/on the right [flank]') indicates the destination; بر + میمنه places the detachment on the right side. The verb فرستاد is finite past, so the agent (he) is understood from context.
segments.s300-g172: گرانمایگان is an appositive/nominal phrase describing the troops as 'the illustrious/noble ones.' The two adjectives/epithets یک‌دل و یک‌تنه are conjoined by و and qualify those men: یک‌دل 'of one heart' (united) and یک‌تنه here idiomatically 'as one/altogether.' Together they emphasize unity and collective resolve rather than literal solitude.
segments.s300-g173: سوی is the preposition 'toward'; میسره here names the other flank/side (an archaising battlefield term — sense not exactly covered by the provided options), and همچنین 'likewise/similarly' links this move to the previous sentence. لشکری is an indefinite noun 'a troop/contingent' (the verb 'sent' is omitted again and understood from the previous clause), so the phrase reads 'toward the ... flank likewise a troop (was sent).'
segments.s300-g174: پراگنده is an adjective/predicate 'scattered/wide‑spread' modifying the troop; بر هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'on/against every side/every direction' (بر + هر + سویی), and مهتری ('a chief' / 'leader') indicates that there was a leader toward each side. The whole segment describes the troop as dispersed across every side with leaders on each direction.
segments.s300-g175: This is a noun string listing the force: سواران ('horsemen') and جنگاوران ('warriors'); the numeral phrase سی هزار (thirty thousand) quantifies them. In epic Persian the order can be inverted for metrical reasons, so the quantity appears after the nouns but still modifies that combined group.
segments.s300-g176: گزیده is a past-participle/adjective 'chosen/selected' qualifying the whole group; همه 'all' emphasizes totality. از در کارزار is a compound prepositional phrase 'from/in the field of battle' (از + در + کارزار) indicating they were picked out of the ranks of fighting men — i.e., chosen veterans for combat.
segments.s300-g9: This phrase begins with the ablative preposition ز + noun phrase (ز قلب سپاه = 'from the heart/core of the army'), then the verb اندر آمد ('came forth/appeared'). The simile چو کوه ('like a mountain') follows the verb as a post-verbal modifier; the verb-subject order and the inverted simile are typical of epic/poetic syntax.
segments.s300-g10: از او گشت here uses از + pronoun او in a causal/agentive sense ('because of him' / 'by him') with گشت (past of گشتن) functioning as a copular/transformative verb 'became/was made'. The following noun phrase جهن دلاور ستوه is the patient/subject: 'Jahn, the brave, (was) weary' — attribute ستوه ('weary') is the predicate and word order is inverted (predicate after the epithet).
segments.s300-g11: سوی راست 'to the right' is an adverbial direction; گستهم is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'turned/moved' (verb before subject, a common epic inversion). نوذر is the subject placed after the verb. چو introduces a temporal clause ('when') that links to the next clause (چو گرد …).
segments.s300-g12: بیامد is past 'came' and is used with the following nominal phrase (subject follows the verb in poetic order). دمان here functions as the coming agent (apparently a name, Daman, though the lexical option given is literal 'skirt'); با درفش 'with (a) standard/banner' modifies his coming, and نبرد ('battle') qualifies the type or purpose of the standard ('battle-standard').
segments.s300-g13: جهان شد 'the world became' is a simple copular construction. ز گرد سواران is an ablative/cause phrase 'from the dust/cloud of the horsemen' and بنفش is the resulting predicate adjective 'purple' placed after the causal phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s300-g14: Two parallel nominal phrases linked by و: زمین پر سپاه ('the ground/full of troops') and هوا پر درفش ('the air/full of banners'). پر is used postpositively to mean 'full (of)' and governs the noun that follows it; the overall structure is a loose verbless description continuing the result begun in the previous clause.
segments.s300-g15: بجنبید is a classical/poetic past form meaning 'moved/stirred' (verb precedes subject here). خسرو is the subject following the verb; ز قلب سپاه is the source/ablative phrase 'from the army's heart' indicating where he moved from.
segments.s300-g16: هم 'also' scopes over the proper name افراسیاب (Afrasiab). اندر آن رزمگاه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in that battlefield.' There is an ellipsis of the verb (the action 'moved/was' is understood from the preceding clause), a common poetic device to avoid repetition.
